questions.text: I feel that the weight I assign to others' suffering is the thread that connects my daily choices to my deepest values.
I regularly audit my habits to see which ones are still serving me.
I'd rather produce a rough first draft quickly and refine it than aim for thoroughness upfront.
I hold the view that taking others' suffering seriously enough to act on it — not just to notice it — is what moral conviction actually means.
I find that surprises — even pleasant ones — send a shock through my system.
I tend to receive compliments and praise with a sense of quiet satisfaction rather than elation.
I hold the view that the obligation to take suffering seriously is one of the few moral principles I would never negotiate.
I hold the view that calm, data-informed analysis of painful situations is the most reliable foundation for decisions that have serious consequences for others.
I find that analytical clarity during emotionally demanding situations has been the quality that most consistently enabled me to achieve outcomes I can be proud of.
I feel most confident when I'm free to improvise and respond in real time.
I find that transitions between emotional states happen abruptly for me, not gradually.
I would rather give a keynote than participate in a panel discussion.
I do not naturally notice the way people position themselves during a photo to signal closeness or distance.
My emotional life has a consistent, predictable quality that I've come to rely on.
I am quick to take ownership of a situation when things go sideways.
I instinctively resist hierarchy and prefer flat collaboration.
I do not automatically sense the social temperature of a new environment.
I believe that remaining emotionally uninvolved during another's crisis enables a quality of judgment that emotional involvement would compromise.
I notice when someone addresses one person by name more frequently than others to draw them in or single them out.
I tend to pick the option that strengthens my personal position, even when alternatives would benefit the group more.
I believe that nurturing my own potential is one of my most important responsibilities.
I take pride in helping existing teams become more effective.
I find that my internal world is quiet and spacious rather than intense and crowded.
I find that deflecting praise and redirecting it to others feels natural and right.
I notice that I feel the emotional weight of news stories as if they were happening to me personally.
I find that self-imposed deadlines feel artificial and don't motivate me.
I feel most confident when I have rehearsed or prepared thoroughly for an event.
I notice the way people edit their stories in real time based on the reactions they are getting.
I pick up on the difference between a sigh of relief and a sigh of frustration even when they sound almost identical.
I feel comfortable operating without a clearly designated leader.
I do not pick up on when someone is putting extra effort into their appearance for a specific social situation.
I place items back in their designated spots immediately after using them.
I generally dictate the terms of a collaboration.
I would walk so someone else could ride.
I hold the view that minimizing my own footprint allows more room for others.
I believe that my taste, my preferences, and my style deserve full expression.
